rulings.Case Title: TYLON J. HUNT VS. CITY & COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA, CALIFORNIA UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE APPEALS BOARD'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default. Defendants California Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board ("CUIAB") and State of California's ("State") motion to vacate the defaults entered on 9/25/17 is GRANTED. The motion is timely because a void default can be vacated at any time. (See CCP 473(d); Westport Oil Co. v. Garrison (1971) 19 Cal.App.3d 974, 978.) The record fails to disclose proper service on either movant. The proof of service dated 11/6/15 indicates that plaintiff attempted to serve the summons and amended complaint on the State, but failed to comply with CCP 415.30 because plaintiff failed to provide two copies of the Notice and Acknowledgement of Receipt and a postage-paid return envelope. (Movants' Request for Judicial Notice, Ex. B.) That proof of service does not indicate proper service on the CUIAB either. (See also Vandeleur Dec.). The other proofs of service only indicate service of the statement of damages on the moving defendants. (Movants' Request for Judicial Notice, Exs. C and D.) In sum, there is no competent evidence that the summons and complaint or amended complaint were properly served on the movants and therefore, the motion is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ALEX CHIN VS. COLE HAAN, LLC, AN UNKNOWN BUSINESS ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Cole Haan Company Store, Llc'S Motion For Summary Adjudication. (Part 2 Of 2) For Tentative Rulings Purposes Only.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT COLE HAAN COMPANY STORE, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. (Part 2 of 2) The court GRANTS summary adjudication as to claims seven and eight [inaccurate wage statements and inaccurate payroll records]. Those practices did not cause a loss of money and there is nothing to restore to Chin and Chin can only recover a penalty. (See Byrd v. Masonite Corp., 215 F.Supp.3d 859, 864 (C.D. Cal. 2016); McKenzie v. Federal Express Corp., 765 F.Supp.2d 1222, 1236). The court GRANTS summary adjudication as to claim 9 [business expenses]. Chin testified that he did not incur any uncompensated business expenses. (Chin Depo., 154:9-25.) Chin testified that he used his cell phone at work, but it appears that he did that for his convenience and Cole Haan did not have a policy that required Chin to use his phone. (Chin Depo., 89:9-13; Berguson Depo., 112:21-113:4.) Chin also fails to show that as a stockroom employee he was required to wear Cole Haan's shoes and was unreimbursed. (Parker Dec., Ex. K.) The court DENIES Cole Haan's motion to summarily adjudicate that Chin is not entitled to injunctive relief on his claims as a former employee. Injunctive relief is not a cause of action-it is a remedy-and the only remedy that is a proper basis for summary adjudication under CCP 437c(f)(1) is a claim for punitive damages. (See DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 419, n.3.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Cole Haan is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of Part 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ALEX CHIN VS. COLE HAAN, LLC, AN UNKNOWN BUSINESS ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT COLE HAAN COMPANY STORE, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Defendant Cole Haan Company Store, LLC's motion for summary adjudication is GRANTED AND DENIED IN PART. (Part 1 of 2) Cole Haan can seek summary adjudication of the distinct UCL claims. The claims are based on discrete sections of the Labor Code and allege separate theories of liability. (See Edward Fineman Co. v. Sup.Ct. (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1115; Lilienthal & Fowler v. Sup.Ct. (1993) 12 Cal.App. 1848, 1854-1855; Mathieu v. Norrell Corp. (2004) 115 CA4th 1174, 1188; Silva v. See's Candy Shops, Inc. (2016) 7 Cal.App.5th 235, 237.) The court DENIES summary adjudication on claim one [failure to pay overtime]. Notwithstanding whether Cole Haan properly calculated overtime by including commissions and bonuses, there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether Cole Haan paid all of the overtime that was owed. (Chin Depo., 147:9-12; 138:8-141:8.) The court DENIES summary adjudication on claims two and three [failure to provide meal and rest periods]. The relief that Chin seeks under Labor 226.7 is fairly characterized as the recovery of a wage and therefore, Chin can obtain restitution. (See Calleros v. Rural Metro of San Diego, Inc. 2017 U.S. Dist. Lexis 120119 (7/31/17); Murphy v. Kenneth Cole Productions, Inc. (2007) 40 Cal.4th 1094.) The court DENIES summary adjudication as to claim 5 [failure to pay wages upon termination]. Chin testified that he routinely worked off the clock and was not paid what was owed. (Chin Depo., 129:18-130:2.) The court GRANTS summary adjudication as to claim six [failure to pay wages per Labor Code 204]. Section 204 cannot support a UCL claim because the only remedy is a penalty. (See Byrd v. Masonite Corp., 215 F.Supp.3d 859, 864 (C.D. Cal. 2016). Chin cannot obtain an injunction on claim six because he is no longer an employee of Cole Haan. (See Price v. Starbucks Corp. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 1136, 1142 n.5.) (End of part 1) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: CARROLL HENRY VS. TIMOTHY ANTHONY BONNICI ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 3, CROSS DEFENDANT CARROLL HENRY'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. rulings.Case Title: THEDA FUNG VS. SUSANA MANCIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Responses Striking Objection Made By Theda Fung
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 2, Defendant Terra Nova Real Estate Services, Inc.'s Motion To Compel Responses Striking Objection Made By Theda Fung. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The moving party shall bring to the hearing Defendant's Document Request, Special Interrogatories and Plaintiff's responses thereto. The Plaintiff concedes the responses were served past the statutory time and urges the objections be dealt with at time of trial through a motion in limine. The statutory penalty for late service of a discovery response is waiver of objections. See: C.C.P. 2031.300(a). The Defendant served a timely notice of motion to compel but failed to comply with Rule of Court 3.1345 requiring preparation of a Separate Statement which is required to obtain the relief sought. The Court's will rule on the motion following review of the discovery pleadings. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: GILBERT PAPAZIAN II VS. KEVIN MICHAEL SULLIVAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Mr. Sullivan To Provide Document Responses To Requests For The Production Of Documents Without Objections, To Produce Responsive Documents And For Sanctions Against Mr. Sullivan And His Attorney Andrew Dimitriou
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 4, Plaintiff Gilbert Papazian's Motion To Compel Mr. Sullivan To Provide Document Responses To Requests For The Production Of Documents Without Objections, To Produce Responsive Documents And For Sanctions Against Mr. Sullivan And His Attorney Andrew Dimitriou. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Plaintiff moves to compel Defendant to provide responses to Plaintiff's Document Request served June 9, 2017 and for sanctions. The parties dispute whether the defendant ever responded to that pleading. Plaintiff denies receiving a responsive pleading. Defendant's opposition to the motion and his counsel's declaration aver the response was served in November 2018. A responsive pleading dated October 3, 2017 with verification dated October 10, 2017 is attached to the opposition pleading. But there is no proof of service. The Defendant's attorney shall produce at the hearing the original responsive pleading with original proof of service attached and the Court will rule on the relief sought. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY HARRIS VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 6, Plaintiff Timothy Harris' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court intends to address the issues raised by this motion in a three-step process. The parties shall first agree or the Court will impose an appropriate Protective Order referable to documents to be produced. Discussion of the Protective Order shall be the subject of the hearing. The parties shall bring to the hearing their proposed versions of a Protective Order. After a Protective Order is in place the Defendant shall have twenty days to produce documents agreed to be produced. Within twenty days following receipt of the documents the parties shall meet and confer to determine document categories that remain open requiring Court adjudication, notifying the Court accordingly. A further hearing date for rulings on objections will be scheduled. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/RBU)

If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion for relief from waiver of objection is granted. The omitted objection to the Form Interrogatory response occurred while Plaintiff was represented by predecessor counsel. All of the conditions of C.C.P. 2030.290 have been met and there is no prejudice shown. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JEREMY GOODIN ET AL VS. JEFFREY VENDLEY ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 24, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT JEFFREY VENDLEY'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Under California's liberalized pleading rules, the complaint adequately states its five causes of action. The factual disputes defendant Vendley raises are not appropriate at the pleading stage. Moreover, a plaintiff may plead causes of action in the alternative, so preemption arguments do not apply here. Vendley's demurrers are OVERRULED in their entirety. Count 1 (Breach of Fiduciary Duty): Defendant asserts that the cause "contains no factual statements." Actually, the cause incorporates many facts by reference - and within statutes of limitations. Count 2 (Intentional Interference With Economic Relations): Plaintiffs Goodin and Urban Flowers, Inc. are adequately pled as separate entities. Counts 3 & 4 (Conversion): Sufficiently pled; particularly rules for pleading fraud do not apply to conversion. Counts 5 (Misappropriation of Trade Secrets): The post-pleading procedure for identifying trade secrets is provided in CCP 2019.210. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Goodin is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
